---
title: "03 · Linear Inequalities"
subtitle: "Equations had one answer; inequalities have a region"
lesson-id: "t2.linear-inequalities"
tier: 2
prerequisites: ["t2.linear-equations-one-var", "t1.negative-numbers"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["research", "ai-ml"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with the symbols **<, >, ≤, ≥**? (less, greater, less-or-equal, greater-or-equal)
3. Comfortable that on the [number line](../../tier-1-foundations/02-number-line/index.qmd),
   "−7 < −2" because −7 is *further left*?
:::

## Touch

Move the slider to find $x$ values that make the inequality true. The
shaded region is the **solution set**.

```{ojs}
viewof xTest = Inputs.range([-10, 10], {value: 5, step: 0.5, label: "Test x:"})
viewof relSel = Inputs.select(["x > 3", "x ≤ −2", "2x + 1 < 7", "−x + 4 ≥ 1"], {value: "2x + 1 < 7", label: "Inequality:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let lhsVal, op, rhsVal, soln;
  switch(relSel) {
    case "x > 3":      lhsVal = xTest;       op = ">"; rhsVal = 3;  soln = (a) => a > 3; break;
    case "x ≤ −2":     lhsVal = xTest;       op = "≤"; rhsVal = -2; soln = (a) => a <= -2; break;
    case "2x + 1 < 7": lhsVal = 2*xTest + 1; op = "<"; rhsVal = 7;  soln = (a) => 2*a + 1 < 7; break;
    case "−x + 4 ≥ 1": lhsVal = -xTest + 4;  op = "≥"; rhsVal = 1;  soln = (a) => -a + 4 >= 1; break;
  }
  const works = soln(xTest);
  const W = 720, H = 130, pad = 30;
  const xMin = -10, xMax = 10;
  const x = v => pad + ((v - xMin) / (xMax - xMin)) * (W - 2 * pad);

  const svg = d3.create("svg").attr("viewBox", `0 0 ${W} ${H}`)
    .attr("style", "width:100%; max-width:760px;");
  // Shaded region
  let shadeStart, shadeEnd, openLeft = false, openRight = false;
  if (relSel === "x > 3") { shadeStart = 3; shadeEnd = xMax; openLeft = true; }
  if (relSel === "x ≤ −2") { shadeStart = xMin; shadeEnd = -2; openRight = false; }
  if (relSel === "2x + 1 < 7") { shadeStart = xMin; shadeEnd = 3; openRight = true; } // x < 3
  if (relSel === "−x + 4 ≥ 1") { shadeStart = xMin; shadeEnd = 3; openRight = false; } // x ≤ 3
  svg.append("rect")
    .attr("x", x(shadeStart)).attr("y", H/2 - 8).attr("width", x(shadeEnd) - x(shadeStart)).attr("height", 16)
    .attr("fill", "#a5d6a7").attr("opacity", 0.7);
  // axis
  svg.append("line").attr("x1", pad).attr("x2", W - pad).attr("y1", H/2).attr("y2", H/2)
    .attr("stroke", "#1f2933").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  for (let v = xMin; v <= xMax; v++) {
    svg.append("line").attr("x1", x(v)).attr("x2", x(v)).attr("y1", H/2 - 5).attr("y2", H/2 + 5)
      .attr("stroke", "#1f2933");
    if (v % 2 === 0) svg.append("text").attr("x", x(v)).attr("y", H/2 + 22)
      .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").text(v);
  }
  // marker
  svg.append("circle").attr("cx", x(xTest)).attr("cy", H/2 - 18).attr("r", 7)
    .attr("fill", works ? "#2e7d32" : "#c62828").attr("stroke", "#fff").attr("stroke-width", 2);
  svg.append("text").attr("x", x(xTest)).attr("y", H/2 - 30)
    .attr("text-anchor", "middle").attr("font-size", "12").attr("font-weight", "bold")
    .attr("fill", works ? "#2e7d32" : "#c62828")
    .text(works ? "TRUE" : "false");
  return svg.node();
}
```

```{ojs}
{
  return html`<div style="text-align:center; font-family:monospace; margin-top:0.5rem;">
    The solution set is the green region. Move the slider — green = your x is in the set.
  </div>`;
}
```

An equation usually picks out *one* number. An inequality picks out a
**range** — every number in some part of the line.

## Wonder

1. The inequality $x > 3$ has solutions 4, 5, 6, ... but also 3.5,
   3.0001, $\pi$. Is **3** itself a solution?
2. $x \ge 3$ and $x > 3$ — what's the difference?
3. **Multiplying both sides of $-2x > 6$ by $-1$:** does $x < ?$ or
   $x > ?$ result? (This is the rule that catches everyone first time.)

## Discover

### Problem 1

Solve $x + 5 > 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x > 4$. Same fundamental move as equations: subtract 5 from both
sides. The inequality sign stays the same direction when you add or
subtract.
</details>

### Problem 2

Solve $3x \le 12$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x \le 4$. Divide both sides by 3 (a *positive* number); inequality
direction is unchanged.
</details>

### Problem 3

Solve $-2x > 6$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Try a value: does $x = -4$ satisfy $-2x > 6$? $-2 \cdot -4 = 8 > 6$ ✓.
Does $x = 0$ satisfy it? $0 > 6$ is false. So the solution must be
*more negative*, not more positive.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$x < -3$.** When you divide (or multiply) both sides by a *negative*
number, **flip the inequality**.

$-2x > 6$, divide by $-2$ (negative!): $x < -3$.

**Why?** Multiplying by $-1$ reflects everything across zero. If
$5 > 3$, then $-5 < -3$ — they swap order. The inequality sign records
"left vs right on the number line", and reflecting flips left and
right.

**This is the only rule of inequalities that differs from equations.**
Burn it in: *negative multiply or divide → flip the sign*.
</details>

### Problem 4

Solve $4x - 7 \ge 2x + 5$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Same as solving an equation, but watch the sign-flip rule. (Here you
won't need it — the coefficient of $x$ stays positive.)
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4x - 2x \ge 5 + 7 \Rightarrow 2x \ge 12 \Rightarrow x \ge 6$.

The solution set: $\{ x : x \ge 6 \}$, the half-line from 6 onward,
including 6 itself.
</details>

### Problem 5

A delivery van weighs **800 kg empty** and can carry packages totalling
at most **1500 kg total weight** (van + cargo). What is the maximum
package weight $w$ it can carry? Write as an inequality and solve.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
"At most 1500" means $\le 1500$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$800 + w \le 1500 \Rightarrow w \le 700$ kg.

Real-world constraints — capacity limits, budget caps, time deadlines
— almost always become inequalities, not equations. Inequalities are
the maths of *constraint*.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
The symbols **<** and **>** were introduced by the English mathematician
**Thomas Harriot** in his posthumous *Artis Analyticae Praxis* (1631).
Earlier writers had used clumsier phrases or ad-hoc notations. The
"open mouth always faces the larger number" mnemonic — small point
toward the small number — is exactly Harriot's design intent. The
non-strict forms **≤** and **≥** came later, when 19th-century
analysts needed to talk about boundaries that were *included*.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
An equation pins down a single point on the number line; an inequality
shades a half-line. Solving means finding *which side* — and the only
move that surprises is multiplying by a negative, which flips the
direction.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **linear inequality** in one variable looks like
$$ax + b \ < \ c, \quad ax + b \le c, \quad ax + b > c, \quad ax + b \ge c.$$

The **solution set** is *every* value of $x$ that makes it true — a
**half-line** on the number line.

**Solving rules:**

- Add or subtract the same number on both sides — *direction unchanged*.
- Multiply or divide both sides by a **positive** number — *direction
  unchanged*.
- Multiply or divide both sides by a **negative** number — **flip the
  inequality**.

The strict inequalities `<, >` *exclude* the boundary point (open
circle on the number line). The non-strict `≤, ≥` *include* it
(filled circle).

:::

> *Same rules as equations, with one twist:*
> *Multiply or divide by a negative — flip the sign.*
> *Strict inequalities exclude the boundary; non-strict include it.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Solve $-3(x + 1) > 6$.

A competent solver:

1. Distribute: $-3x - 3 > 6$.
2. Add 3: $-3x > 9$.
3. Divide by $-3$ — **flip!** — : $x < -3$.

**Answer: $x < -3$**, the half-line strictly less than $-3$.

Check with one value: $x = -5 < -3$ ✓. $-3(-5 + 1) = -3 \cdot -4 = 12 >
6$ ✓.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Solve: $x - 4 < 2$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x < 6$.
</details>

**W2.** Solve: $5x \ge 35$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x \ge 7$.
</details>

**W3.** Solve: $-x \le 3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Multiply both sides by $-1$ — flip!
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$x \ge -3$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Solve $2x + 7 < 19$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2x < 12 \Rightarrow x < 6$.
</details>

**P2.** Solve $-4x + 1 \ge 9$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Subtract 1; then divide by $-4$ (flip!).
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$-4x \ge 8 \Rightarrow x \le -2$.
</details>

**P3.** A taxi charges Rs. 30 base + Rs. 15/km. You have **Rs. 200**.
What is the maximum distance you can travel?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$30 + 15d \le 200$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$30 + 15d \le 200 \Rightarrow 15d \le 170 \Rightarrow d \le \tfrac{170}{15}
\approx 11.33$ km.

Round *down* (you can't pay for fractional km in this scenario): max
**11 km**, leaving Rs. 5 unused.

The "round down" choice came from real-world context, not algebra. The
algebra gave the bound; you picked the integer.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Solve the *compound* inequality $-3 < 2x + 1 \le 7$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Solve "$-3 < 2x + 1$" and "$2x + 1 \le 7$" — both must hold.
Equivalently, do operations on *all three* parts simultaneously.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Subtract 1 from all three parts: $-4 < 2x \le 6$. Divide by 2: $-2 < x
\le 3$.

The solution is the *interval* $(-2, 3]$ — strictly greater than $-2$,
up to and including $3$. Compound inequalities express **bounded
ranges** — the natural way to write "between $a$ and $b$".
</details>

**S2.** A neural network's *prediction loss* must be a non-negative
number — and ideally as small as possible. We want $L(w) < 0.01$ where
$L$ depends on the model's weights $w$. We don't know $L$ explicitly,
but we know $L(w) \ge 0$ for all $w$. What does the inequality $0 \le
L(w) < 0.01$ describe in plain words?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
"The loss is non-negative AND less than 0.01" — i.e., the loss is in
the interval $[0, 0.01)$. This is the *acceptance condition* for a
trained model: training succeeds when the loss lies in this region.

Inequalities (and their generalisation, **constraints**) are the
language of optimisation problems — and ML training is the largest
optimisation problem the world has ever computed.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Inequalities have one answer."** No — they have a *region*.
   Multiple values (often infinitely many) satisfy them.
2. **"The inequality always points the same way after manipulation."**
   No — multiplying or dividing by a *negative* number flips it.
3. **"Strict and non-strict are the same."** $x > 3$ excludes $x = 3$;
   $x \ge 3$ includes it. The boundary matters in constraints —
   especially in optimisation.
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **A [linear inequality](/glossary.qmd#linear-inequality) picks out a
   region on the number line, not a single value.**
2. **Solve it like an equation, with one new rule: multiplying or
   dividing by a negative number flips the inequality sign.**
3. **The world's "at most" and "at least" wording almost always hides
   an inequality — they are the maths of constraint.**

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you explain *why* multiplying both sides by a negative flips
   the inequality, using the number-line picture?
2. Without scrolling back, can you solve $-2x + 5 < 11$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $-2x < 6 \Rightarrow x > -3$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[04 · The Coordinate Plane](../04-coordinate-plane/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (compound inequalities).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3* — the sign-flip rule is *the*
  inequality-specific gotcha.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[02 · Linear Equations](../02-linear-equations-one-var/index.qmd)** —
  same fundamental move; same strategy.
- **[T1 L10 — Negative Numbers](../../tier-1-foundations/10-negative-numbers/index.qmd)** —
  the sign-flip rule comes from how negation reflects across zero.

### What this unlocks

- **Tier 3 — Quadratic Inequalities** — same idea, parabolic regions.
- **Tier 6 — Linear Programming & Optimisation** — entire systems of
  inequalities defining feasible regions.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Constraint satisfaction is the heart of
optimisation. Linear *programming* (an entire branch of operations
research) handles thousands of inequalities at once. Modern ML uses
constraint-based formulations everywhere.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Everywhere else.** Budgets ("don't spend more than X"), capacity
limits, deadlines, regulatory thresholds, fitness goals ("at least X
steps per day") — the moment a problem says "at most" or "at least", an
inequality is hiding in the wording.
:::
